coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of perovskite nanocrystals into lanthanide metal-organic frameworks with enhanced stability for ratiometric and visual sensing of mercury in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Yun
Ye, Qiuyu
Dai, Tao
Guan, Jie
Ji, Zhengping
Xu, Qin
Hu, Xiaoya
coredata.dc:description: In-situ growth of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal into Eu-BTC was realized for synthesis of dual-emission CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC by a facile solvothermal method, and a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was prepared for rapid, sensitive and visual detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis were used to verify the successful incorporation of CsPbBr3 into the Eu-BTC. Meanwhile, the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite maintained high fluorescence performance and stability in aqueous solution. After adding Hg2+, the green fluorescence of CsPbBr3 was quenched and the red fluorescence of Eu3+ remained unchanged, while the color changed from green to red obviously. The occurrence of dynamic quenching and electron transfer were verified by fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer quenching constant and XPS analysis. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor shows high analytical performance for Hg2+ detection with a wide linear range of 0–1 μM and a low detection limit of 0.116 nM. In addition, it also shows high selectivity for the detection of Hg2+ and can be successfully applied to detect Hg2+ in environmental water samples. More importantly, a novel paper-based sensor based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC ratiometric probe was successfully manufactured for the visual detection of Hg2+ by naked eyes. This new type of ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows great potential for applications in point-of-care diagnostics.

The decay curves are excited at 260 nm.

(A) Emission spectra of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC responding to Hg2+ with various concentration. (B) The fitting curve between I520/I616 and the Hg2+ concentration. head.ce:title: Incorporation of perovskite nanocrystals into lanthanide metal-organic frameworks with enhanced stability for ratiometric and visual sensing of mercury in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In-situ growth of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal into Eu-BTC was realized for synthesis of dual-emission CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC by a facile solvothermal method, and a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was prepared for rapid, sensitive and visual detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis were used to verify the successful incorporation of CsPbBr3 into the Eu-BTC. Meanwhile, the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite maintained high fluorescence performance and stability in aqueous solution. After adding Hg2+, the green fluorescence of CsPbBr3 was quenched and the red fluorescence of Eu3+ remained unchanged, while the color changed from green to red obviously. The occurrence of dynamic quenching and electron transfer were verified by fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer quenching constant and XPS analysis. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor shows high analytical performance for Hg2+ detection with a wide linear range of 0–1 μM and a low detection limit of 0.116 nM. In addition, it also shows high selectivity for the detection of Hg2+ and can be successfully applied to detect Hg2+ in environmental water samples. More importantly, a novel paper-based sensor based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC ratiometric probe was successfully manufactured for the visual detection of Hg2+ by naked eyes. This new type of ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows great potential for applications in point-of-care diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: The indiscriminate discharge of heavy metal mercury ion has caused damage to the ecosystem and posed a great threat to the human health (Rasheed et al., 2018a; 2018c; Rasheed et al., 2019a). To ensure the safety of water samples in the environment and food, it is vital to develop rapid, low-cost, sensitive and easy-to-operation methods for Hg2+ detection. The commonly used analytical methods for Hg2+ detection including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Sabouri and Nouroozi, 2016), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Petry-Podgórska et al., 2021), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Li et al., 2019), electrochemical methods (Pang et al., 2021) and etc. have several limitations such as being time-consuming, expensive instrumentation and complicated sample preparation. Fluorescent sensors have attracted considerable attention for mercury ions detection due to their rapid response, distinct visualization, easy operation, low cost and so on (Cui et al., 2015; Rasheed et al., 2018b; 2018d; Rasheed et al., 2019b). To date, various fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots (Cui et al., 2015), quantum dots (Shu et al., 2021c), luminescent molecular dyes (Rasheed et al., 2018a; 2018b; 2018c; 2018d; 2018e), and luminescent metal nanocrystals (Zhang and Yu, 2014) have been synthesized and used for the design of fluorescent sensors for mercury detection. However, most of them can only detect a single fluorescence signal, leading to poor detection accuracy due to uncertain experimental factors and interference of environmental media. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors which detect the analyte using the ratio of two or more emission bands at different wavelengths as analytical signal, eliminating influence that originate from concentration, environment and excitation intensity that provide improved sensitivity and accuracy. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescence method can realize efficient visual detection of analytes by triggering changes in different fluorescence color types. Visual detection has the advantages of low cost, short assay time and do not require skilled technicians in detection, so as to realize rapid and on-site analysis (Jia et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Xu and Yan, 2016a; ).

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), prepared by self-assembled between various metal centers and organic ligands, have attracted much attention due to their high specific surface areas, structural diversity, controllable pore sizes and great thermal stability (Cai et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021; Shu et al., 2021b). As an important subclass of MOF materials, luminescent MOFs can be used for chemical sensors of metal ions, anions, antibiotics and explosives detection due to the well-defined porosity and tunable emission properties (Helal et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2016; Rasheed and Nabeel, 2019; Shu et al., 2021a; Wei et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2016). At present, two main strategies have been adopted in order to synthetize luminescent MOFs. One strategy is using lanthanide ions as the metal center to synthesize lanthanide-MOFs (Ln-MOFs). The efficient ligand-metal energy transfer which is the so-called “antenna effect” makes the Ln-MOFs possess long luminescence lifetimes and high quantum yields (Rocha et al., 2011). Another strategy is to utilize the tunable pore size and shape of MOF materials to encapsulate luminescent guest molecules or nanocrystals into the MOFs framework (Shu et al., 2021d). The MOF materials have the advantages of tunable porosity, wide binding possibility, and controllable and orderly arrangement, which makes them become a good matrix for the incorporation of luminescent guests. Combining the excellent fluorescence characteristics of Ln-MOF and its inherent porosity and guest binding ability, it is necessary to explore another fluorescent nanomaterial and introduce it into the channels or cavities of Ln-MOF to form a new type of ratiometric fluorescence sensor to enhance the accuracy of the signal response to the analyte.

At present, doping carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into Ln-MOF to construct dual-emission sensors for detection of various metal ions have been reported. For example, Zhang et al., (2021) encapsulated formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots (FA-CDs) in the cavity of Eu-MOF, rationally designed and synthesized a ratio fluorescent probe for detecting Ag+, which has the advantages of good selectivity and low detection limit. Xu and Yan, 2016a encapsulated CDs with fluorescence activity into MOF-253 and adsorbed rare earth ions (Eu3+) to form Eu3+/CdS@MOF-253 dual luminescent material for detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution with a wide detection range and a detection limit as low as 13 nM. However, CQDs requires surface passivation to maintain high quantum yield, and synthesis takes a high temperature and a long time. In contrast, the emerging perovskite nanocrystals are highly tolerant of defects and therefore possess high photoluminescence quantum yields. And because of its simple synthesis, tunable emission, high color purity and excellent carrier characteristics, it has attracted much attention (Chen et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2021; Guan et al., 2021a; Guan et al., 2021b; Jeon et al., 2015). The luminescent properties of perovskite quantum dots can cover the whole visible light region by adjusting the crystal size and chemical composition (Zhang et al., 2015). Doping perovskite as a luminescent guest into luminescent MOFs can construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors with different emission combinations according to the characteristics of the diversity of fluorescence emission from these two materials. Liu et al., (2020) doped CsPbBr3 into the luminescent MOF by one-pot method to construct a ratiometric probe with luminescence of CsPbBr3 at 528 nm and the MOF at 618 nm. The two emission centers had different luminescence behaviors in the temperature range of 20–100 ℃, and successfully constructed a ratio luminescence thermometer. Zhang et al., (2018) prepared CH3NH3PbBr3@Eu-MOF hybrid materials using a two-step method, and prepared hybrid materials with multilevel anti-counterfeiting behavior according to the light-emitting properties of the composite materials that were dependent on excitation wavelength and temperature. However, the currently reported works of perovskite doped MOF materials for luminescent sensing are still limited. This is mainly because the combination strategy and incorporation mechanism of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals and luminescent MOFs are still challenging, and the poor fluorescence and water stability in aqueous solutions remains the biggest challenge for their practical sensing applications.

In the present work, we report for the first time the introduction of all inorganic perovskite nanocrystals into lanthanide MOFs via an in-situ synthesis method to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence and stability of the nanocomposites in water were studied. Utilizing the abundant nitrogen-containing groups in surface ligands of CsPbBr3 to achieve specific fluorescence response to Hg2+, and using Eu-BTC as the carrier and internal standard to construct a self-calibration system based on CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, which can overcome environmental interference or instrument errors. The quenching mechanism was further investigated by fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer quenching constant and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. This ratiometric sensor exhibited good analytical performance for Hg2+ detection with a low detection limit of 0.116 nM and wide linear range of 0–1 μM. More importantly, the paper device was prepared based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanoprobe can realize the detection of Hg2+ by the naked eyes. This study not only developed a novel and simple ratio fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection, but also opens up an effective way for the development of visual fluorescence sensor for point-of-care testing.

1,3,5-trimesic acid (H3BTC, 98%, Aladdin), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (99.9%, Aladdin), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), lead bromide (PbBr2, 99%, Aladdin), cesium bromide (CsBr, 99%, Aladdin), oleylamine (OAm, 90%, McLean). All reagents were used without any further purification.

The fluorescence emission spectrum was scanned by UV–vis/near-infrared fluorescence spectrometer FS5 (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd.). UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed on a UV2550 spectrometer (SHIMADZU). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured on the JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was surveyed by an Antaris II Fourier transform nearinfrared spectrometer. X-ray diffractometer D8ADVANCE (Bruker Co) was used for XRD analysis. The material was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. FLS 1000 Fluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd.) has measured the decay of the fluorescence lifetime. Determination of specific surface area by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (Rubothem, Germany). Inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-MS) was used to determine ion content (PE company, USA).

The Eu-BTC composite was prepared by placing 446 mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, 210 mg H3BTC, 40 mL DMF and 10 mL ethanol in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacting at 120 ℃ for 2 days. After the reaction, the products were separated by centrifugation and washed with DMF/ethanol for several times. Then it was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ℃ for 12 h.

85 mg CsBr and 147 mg PbBr 2 were dissolved in 10 mL DMF and stirred until complete dissolution to obtain the CsPbBr3 precursor solution. 1 mL of the CsPbBr3 precursor solution was added with 5 mg Eu-BTC, 50 μL OAm, 2 mg amphiphilic octylamine-modified poly (acrylic acid) (OPA) polymers synthesized according to previous reports (He et al., 2011) and stirred for 5 min at room temperature. Finally, after washing twice with toluene by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 5 min and drying in vacuum at 60 ℃ for 2 h, they were taken out for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assays of Hg2+ by CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC
ce:section.ce:para: First, 3246 mg of mercury nitrate was weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of nitric acid (1 mol/L) solution, and the volume was adjusted to 1000 mL with ultrapure water to obtain a 0.01 mol/L mercury ion aqueous solution. The 0.01 mol/L Hg2+ solution was then diluted into a series of Hg2+ standard solutions with ultrapure water. 60 mg of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC powder was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water, and ultrasonic was performed for 5 min to make the composite evenly dispersed. 900 μL CsPbBr3 @Eu-BTC stock solution was added to freshly prepared Hg2+ solutions and mixed to give final concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 μM. The luminescence peaks at 520 nm and 616 nm of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC after addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ were recorded under 260 nm excitation, and the colors of the samples were observed under UV lamp. Other metal ions detection experiments were the same as the above operation process to determine the selectivity of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC to Hg2+. All tests were repeated 3 times at room temperature (20 ℃).

In order to verify the feasibility of the sensor in detecting Hg2+ in actual water samples, the Hg2+ in lake water and domestic sewage was determined by using CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanoprobe. The lake water was obtained from the Slender West Lake of Yangzhou. The water sample was centrifuged and the impurities were removed with a filter membrane. 30 mg CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC powder was dispersed in 10 mL of actual water sample. Ultrasonic treatment was conducted for 5 min to disperse the composite evenly. 900 μL of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC actual water samples were added with 100 μL of Hg2+ solutions of different concentrations (0, 50, 300, 500 nM), and the actual water proportion in the mixture is 0.9. The luminescence peaks of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC at 520 nm and 616 nm after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ under 260 nm excitation were recorded, and the concentration of Hg2+ was calculated according to the standard curve of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC detection system. The recovery rate of Hg2+ based on CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC detection system was calculated from measured concentration and standard concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the paper device based on the ratio fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: A simple wax screen printing technique was used to make a paper device. Wax screen printing was accomplished by printing a pattern of solid wax on the filter paper surface through a patterned screen. After printing, scrape off the wax residue with a ruler. The wax covering the filter paper was melted after 10 s with a hot hair dryer. Then remove the filter paper from the design screen, and then dried for 5 s to produce a hydrophobic channel on the filter paper. A row of round holes paper device with holes of 6 mm diameter locating 8.0 mm apart were prepared. Then dropped CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC aqueous solution (about 3 mg/mL) into the prepared paper device and dried it naturally at room temperature. When detecting Hg2+, different concentrations of Hg2+ were dropped on the paper device and dried in air.

In this work, dual-emission fluorescent CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite was prepared by in-situ growth of perovskite in the Eu-BTC MOFs using a facile solvothermal method. The morphology of the synthesized Eu-BTC and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, Eu-BTC displayed the rod-shaped structure with a uniform length of about 1.5 µm. After synthesis of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC composite, CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC maintained the original rod-shaped morphology of Eu-BTC, while CsPbBr3 nanocrystals were uniformly distributed on the surface of Eu-BTC, showing good mono-dispersity property without apparent aggregation (Fig. 1B). It may be that the MOF material acts as a good matrix to embed CsPbBr3 nanocrystals, protecting the CsPbBr3 nanocrystals from combining with the adjacent CsPbBr3 nanocrystals. Meantime, the crystal structure of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposites was analyzed by XRD. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared Eu-BTC and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposites were shown in Fig. 1C. The XRD pattern of Eu-BTC (red line) was in good agreement with the simulated Eu-BTC crystals, indicating the high purity of the synthesized Eu-BTC (Yang et al., 2006). The XRD diffraction peaks of the as-prepared CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC composites can be indexed to those of the simulated XRD pattern of CsPbBr3 (PDF#54–0752) and Eu-BTC (CCDC No. 290771). The characteristic diffraction peaks of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals and Eu-BTC can be accurately observed in CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, such as the new peaks at 15.2°, 21.6°, 30.6° and 37.8° could be attributed to the (100), (110), (200) and (211) planes of newly formed CsPbBr3, further indicating the successful doping of CsPbBr3 into Eu-BTC and the doping process did not destroy the integrity of the Eu-BTC crystal.

The surface functional groups of Eu-BTC, CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC were characterized by FTIR analysis (Fig. 1D). Eu-BTC has strong adsorption bands in the range of 1635–1546, 1454–1390 and 792–728 cm−1, which were respectively related to the carboxylate groups, skeletal vibrations and bending vibrations of benzene ring. With regard to CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, all of these characteristic adsorption bands are consistent with Eu-BTC except adsorption bands at 3200, 2923 and 2854 cm−1. The peak observed near 3200 cm−1 is assigned to a N-H stretching vibration, the other two characteristic adsorption peaks can be attributed to C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations, which were both from OAm and OPA molecules anchored on the surface of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals. Definitely indicating that CsPbBr3 had been firmly incorporated into Eu-BTC. To determine the elemental components and valence state of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposites, the XPS analysis was shown in Fig. 1E–I and Fig. S1A-S1C. The characteristic peaks of Eu 3d (1134.3 eV), Cs 3d (724.3 eV), Pb 4 f (137.4 eV), Br 3d (67.8 eV), C 1 s (284.3 eV), N 1 s (398.5 eV), and O 1 s (531.8 eV) appeared in the wide-scan XPS spectrum. Among them, Eu 3d showed two peaks of Eu 3d3/2 and Eu 3d5/2, corresponding to 1164.3 eV and 1134.6 eV respectively (Fig. S1A). Cs 3d showed two peaks of Cs 3d3/2, Cs 3d5/2 and Br 3d showed two peaks of Br 3d3/2, Br 3d5/2 (Fig. S1B-S1C). In addition, compared with CsPbBr3, the binding energy of Pb 4 f shifted negatively after Eu-BTC coating (Fig. 1F), which might be originate from the strong interaction between Pb2+ and organic ligands, thus indicating the close contact between quantum dots and metal organic framework (Pu et al., 2017). Fig. 1G, H and I showed C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s bands of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposites, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s could be fitted into three peaks at 288.4, 285.1 and 284.6 eV, attributed to O-CO, C-N and C-H/CC groups (Fig. 1G). In N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1H), three bands at 401.9, 400.8, 399.5 eV were assigned to N-Pb, N-H, N-C groups, among them, the N-Pb bond belongs to the coordination bond between -NH2 and Pb ions. The spectra of O1s exhibited two peaks at 532.1 and 531.3 eV, which were attributed to the O-CO and OC groups. The results of XPS analysis further verified CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite was successfully prepared and the existence of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the nanocomposite. Fig. S2 shows the quantitative study of the pore structure of Eu-BTC and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) N2 gas adsorption method. According to the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, the pore size distribution is calculated. As shown in Table S1, compared with Eu-BTC, the specific surface area, pore volume and pore diameter of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC are all correspondingly reduced, which may be that CsPbBr3 were absorbed on the surface and pores of Eu-BTC crystals through the electrostatic interaction. The reduction of the surface area may be that the adsorption of CsPbBr3 onto the surface and pores of Eu-BTC resulted in the decrease of the pore size and volume. The results proved that Eu-BTC was successfully used as a matrix to incorporate CsPbBr3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties and stability of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the fluorescent properties of the composite material CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, the fluorescence spectra of CsPbBr3, Eu-BTC and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC were recorded. As shown in Fig. S3A, the composite material showed three emission peaks at 520 nm, 593 nm and 616 nm respectively, which corresponded to the emission of CsPbBr3 and the emission of Eu-BTC from 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2 electronic transition of Eu3+. Next, the amount ratio of MOF and perovskite was verified to investigate its influence on the fluorescence properties of the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite, the fluorescence spectra of the nanocomposite after adding different amounts of Eu-BTC during the synthesis process were recorded. As shown in Fig. S3B, the emission peak of Eu-BTC at 616 nm gradually enhanced and that of CsPbBr3 at 520 nm was basically unchanged with the increasing amount of Eu-BTC (0–20 mg), indicating that after CsPbBr3 was incorporated into Eu-BTC, the fluorescence properties of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals were retained and the nanocomposite could have two different emission peaks simultaneously. In addition, the excitation wavelength of the composite material was optimized by recording the fluorescence spectrum of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC at the excitation wavelength of 240–320 nm. It can be seen from Fig. S3C that the fluorescence peak of perovskite increased with the wavelength changed from 240 to 320 nm, while the fluorescence peak of Eu-BTC decreased with the wavelength increased and almost no fluorescence was observed when the excitation wavelength was greater than 300 nm. In order to construct a ratiometric sensor, the excitation wavelength was chosen to be 260 nm for subsequent Hg2+ detection.

To investigate the potential of the composite for practical sensing applications, the stability of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC solution in different pH and water environments was further studied. The fluorescence intensity ratio of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC (I520/I616) kept stable over a wide pH range (4−9) (Fig. S4A). In addition, XRD and ICP analysis of the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC samples after storage in aqueous solution for different time was performed (Fig. S4B, S4C). The total lead ion content in the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC samples was measured by the ICP-MS. It is found that the corresponding XRD peaks of the material was basically unchanged after storage in water, and the content of Pb2+ maintained 90% of the original value in CsPbBr3 for 10 d, showing that the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposite can maintain the high stability in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensing mechanism of Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the ratiometric fluorescence properties of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC nanocomposites, we explored the feasibility of detecting mercury in aqueous solutions. The CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC displayed bright fluorescence and both characteristic emission of CsPbBr3 and Eu-BTC in aqueous solution under a single excitation of 260 nm ( Fig. 2A). When Hg2+ was added to the probe stock solution, the emission of CsPbBr3 at 520 nm decreased, and the corresponding fluorescence quantum yield decreased from 57.33% (compared with fluorescein R6G, 95%) to 15.78%. The emission peak intensity and quantum yield of Eu-BTC at 616 nm remained unchanged (Fig. S5, Table S2). At this time, the solution emitted red fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 2A), the inset showed the corresponding images under UV light, and the corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram shows the color change obviously in Fig. 2B. In addition, Hg2+ was added to the samples synthesized at different amount of Eu-BTC in aqueous solution, and the fluorescence color changes of the solution before and after addition of Hg2+ were compared (Fig. S6). It was found that the color change from green to red was observed most obvious when the addition amount of Eu-BTC was 5 mg during the synthesis. Therefore, in order to achieve a visual color tonality change, the sample synthesized at this ratio was selected for subsequent detection applications.

The sensing mechanism of Hg2+ detection by this ratiometric probe was further studied. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be divided into four main categories, including the formation of ground state complexes, energy resonance transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtration effect (IFE). Since the absorption peak of Hg2+ did not overlap with the excitation and emission of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, the quenching effect caused by IFE and FRET were excluded first. Secondly, the UV–vis absorption spectra were measured, The UV–vis absorption spectra of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC in the absence and presence of Hg2+ remained basically unchanged (Fig. S7). Therefore, the static quenching mechanism caused by the coordination of Hg2+ and CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC surface groups to form non-luminescent complexes can be ruled out (Wu et al., 2014). The XRD patterns of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC before and after the addition of Hg2+ showed that the diffraction peaks of the composites did not change significantly after the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S8), which indicated that CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC maintained the integrity of the crystal structure, indicating that the fluorescence quenching was related to the collapse of the skeleton is irrelevant. In addition, the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC with the increase of Hg2+ concentration at different temperatures were recorded (Fig. S9), and the results were analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation to further study the quenching mechanism. The equation for Stern-Volmer is presented by (1):

In this equation, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity ratios (I520/I616) of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC at 520 nm and 616 nm in the presence or absence of Hg2+, respectively. K
sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of quencher Hg2+. The slope of the calibration curve representing the Stern-Volmer constant is 0.00106, 0.00149, and 0.00176 at 288 K, 298 K, and 308 K, respectively, and the correlation coefficients (R2) are all greater than 0.98 (Table S3). The K
sv value increased with the increase of temperature, indicating that the quenching process is a dynamic quenching process (He et al., 2014). Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetimes of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC with various concentrations of Hg2+ were further measured to investigate the quenching mechanism (Fig. 2C and D). The average lifetimes of CsPbBr3 and Eu-BTC were calculated according to the following formula respectively (2):

τ1, τ2 and τ3 are the lifetimes of three different fluorescence emission pathways, while A1, A2 and A3 represent their respective relative weights at t = 0. In the presence of 10 nM and 100 nM Hg2+, the average lifetime of CsPbBr3 dropped from 14.09 μs to 10.76 μs and 8.73 μs, respectively, while there is almost no change in the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-BTC (Table S4). The decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of the perovskite is attributed to the strong electrostatic adsorption or metal-ligand coordination between the perovskite and Hg2+, resulting in electron or energy transfer from the conduction band of the quantum dot to the metal ion. The transfer process causes the excitons to decay faster and the fluorescence lifetime become shorter (Chen et al., 2020; Doose et al., 2009). XPS was used to further study the interaction mechanism between perovskite and Hg2+, compared with the original CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC, the peaks of C 1 s and O 1 s have no obvious changes (Fig. S10A, S10B), which indicates that there is little interaction between Hg2+ ions and carbon and oxygen atoms on the surface of the perovskite ligand. The position of the N 1 s peak of the nitrogen atom was shifted after adding Hg2+ and a new peak was formed at 402.33 eV (Fig. S10C, S10D), which can be attributed to the new coordination bond N-Hg formed between the nitrogen atom in the surface ligand of CsPbBr3 and Hg2+. Therefore, the quenching mechanism was attributed to the non-radiative electron/hole recombination and annihilation through the effective electron transfer process caused by the high coordination effect between the nitrogen element in the surface ligand of CsPbBr3 and Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC based ratiometric sensor for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC ratio probe to Hg2+, a variety of cations and anions including Hg2+ (1 μM), Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co3+, Cd2+, F-, Cl-, I-, NO3 -, PO4 3-, H2PO4 - (200 μM) were added to the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC solution (Tris·HCl, pH = 7.0), and the fluorescence change was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3A, only Hg2+ has a fluorescence quenching effect on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC obviously and there was an obvious change of fluorescence color under UV lamp (Fig. S11A), while other metal ions have very weak or almost no fluorescence changes. In addition, an interferent experiment was carried out by adding Hg2+ to the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC mixed solution containing the above-mentioned interfering ions. Among them, the concentration of interference ions was 200 times higher than that of mercury ions. As shown in Fig. 3B and Fig. S11B, after adding of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity ratio decreased significantly and the quenching efficiency was close to that of adding only Hg2+, demonstrating that the presence of these metal ions did not affect the detection of Hg2+. Overall, it demonstrated that CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC is an ideal candidate for construction of a highly selective ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection.

In addition, to obtain the highest sensitivity of the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC based ratiometric sensor for Hg2+ detection, the possible basic parameters might affect the reaction process including the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC concentration, reaction pH and time were optimized. Fig. S12A shows the fluorescence intensity ratio (I520/I616) of different concentrations of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC before and after the addition of the same concentration of Hg2+, and the results showed that the response signal was most obvious when the concentration was 3 mg/mL. The effect of pH on the binding study of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC with Hg2+ was investigated (Fig. S12B). It was found that the fluorescence decreased after addition of Hg2+ in the pH range of 4–9 and there was no obvious difference of the fluorescence change at different pH, demonstrating the influence of pH on the binding ability could be almost neglected. Since the fluorescence intensity of the CsPbBr3 maintained higher in the pH range of 6–7, the subsequent detection was performed under the pH of 7. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I520/I616) of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC after reacting with Hg2+ for 0–60 min was also recorded. As show in Fig. S12C, the fluorescence of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC can maintain very stable for at least 60 min in aqueous solution. After the addition of Hg2+, the perovskite fluorescence intensity at 520 nm decreased rapidly and obviously, and then the fluorescence intensity ratio remained stable. It illustrated that the detection of Hg2+ by the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC material is essentially independent of the reaction time, thereby achieving rapid detection of Hg2+.

To evaluate the potential of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe, different concentrations of Hg2+ (0 ~ 10 μM) were added to investigate its effects on the fluorescence properties of CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A that the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm of CsPbBr3 is gradually weakened with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, while the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm of Eu-BTC has little change. The fluorescence intensity ratio I520/I616 displayed a good linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 ~ 1 μM with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9785 (Fig. 4B). The detection limit was estimated to be 0.116 nM. Compared with other fluorescent nanomaterials-based sensors, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor showed high analytical performance with a wider linear range and a higher sensitivity ( Table 1). It is attributed to the red emitting Eu-BTC serves as an ideal reference to overcome the influences of excitation light intensity and environmental interference. Moreover, the ratiometric sensor can realize visual recognition ability for Hg2+.

Furthermore, a portable and low-cost visual probe based paper device is developed in order to realize rapid and simple semi quantitative detection of Hg2+. CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was dropped into the prepared paper device and dried it naturally at room temperature. Subsequently, different concentrations of Hg2+ standard solution were added to verify its visual sensing performance. As shown in Fig. 4C, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 ~ 10 μM, the fluorescent color of the paper device gradually changes from green to yellow, orange and finally to red under the irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp, which is easy to be recognized by the naked eye and convenient for point-of care testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Hg2+ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The content of Hg2+ in actual water samples (lake water and domestic sewage) was measured by spiking and recovery experiments to evaluate the feasibility of the developed CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection in actual water samples. Different concentrations of Hg2+ (50, 300 and 500 nM) were added to the above water samples, respectively. As shown in Table 2, the satisfactory recoveries range of 97.63–101.18%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.5–2.9% were achieved for Hg2+ analysis, the results indicate that CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC based ratiometric fluorescent sensor is very promising in the accurate detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

In summary, a facile solvothermal method was used for in-situ growth of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals into the Eu-BTC, and a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was constructed for sensitive and visual detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution by naked eyes. The successful incorporation of CsPbBr3 into the Eu-BTC was verified by the TEM, XRD, XPS and BET analysis. The ratiometric CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC fluorescent probe showed good fluorescence performance and stability in aqueous solution. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence of CsPbBr3 at 520 nm was quenched through a dynamic quenching and electron transfer mechanism, while the red fluorescence of Eu-BTC at 616 nm showed no change, thus provided a self-calibrating sensing system. Meantime, the change of fluorescence color from green to red was obviously observed by naked eye when detecting Hg2+. In addition, the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC fluorescent probe achieved rapid, highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a wide linear relationship in the range of 0 ~ 1 μM and a low detection limit of 0.116 nM. It has also been successfully applied to monitor Hg2+ in lake water and domestic sewage with high accuracy and recovery. Furthermore, a novel paper device based on the CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC was successfully manufactured for visual detection of Hg2+ by naked eyes. This study not only developed a novel strategy for incorporation of CsPbBr3 into the Eu-BTC to obtain the dual-emission CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC with high fluorescence and water-stability, but also provided an effective pathway for development of visual fluorescent sensors in point-of-care diagnostics.

Xiaoya Hu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.
